perturbation.original_text: f. Force Majeure. In the event that either party is prevented from engaging in the marketing and promotional activities in Exhibit A manufacturing, distributing or selling the Licensed Property because of any act of God; unavoidable accident; fire, epidemic; strike, lockout, or other labor dispute; war, riot or civil commotion; act of public enemy; enactment of any rule, law, order or act of government or governmental instrumentality (whether federal, state, local or foreign); or other cause beyond such party's control, and such condition continues for a period of two (2) months or more, either party hereto shall have the right to terminate this Agreement effective at any time during the continuation of such condition by giving the other party at least thirty (30) days' notice to such effect. In such event, all payments made shall become immediately due and payable and this Agreement shall be automatically terminated.
perturbation.changed_text: f. Force Majeure. In the event that either party is prevented from engaging in the marketing and promotional activities in Exhibit A manufacturing, distributing or selling the Licensed Property because of any act of God; unavoidable accident; fire, epidemic; strike, lockout, or other labor dispute; war, riot or civil commotion; act of public enemy; enactment of any rule, law, order or act of government or governmental instrumentality (whether federal, state, local or foreign); or other cause beyond such party's control, and such condition continues for a period of two (2) months or more, either party hereto shall have the right to terminate this Agreement effective at any time during the continuation of such condition by giving the other party at least thirty (30) days' notice to such effect.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the sentence 'In such event, all payments made shall become immediately due and payable and this Agreement shall be automatically terminated,' the contract omits the requirement for immediate payment upon termination due to force majeure. This creates a potential contradiction with California law regarding contract termination and payment obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1692 addresses the restoration of benefits upon rescission of a contract. While 'force majeure' is not explicitly 'rescission,' the effect of the original clause is similar: the contract terminates, and outstanding payments become due. By removing the automatic payment clause, the modified contract could be interpreted as allowing a party to avoid making payments for services already rendered before the force majeure event, potentially conflicting with the principle of restoring benefits as intended by California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. EXTINCTION OF CONTRACTS [1682 - 1701] ( Title 5 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Rescission [1688 - 1693] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) 1692. When a contract has been rescinded in whole or in part, any party to the contract may seek relief based upon such rescission by (a) bringing an action to recover any money or thing owing to him by any other party to the contract as a consequence of such rescission or for any other relief to which he may be entitled under the circumstances or (b) asserting such rescission by way of defense or cross-complaint. If in an action or proceeding a party seeks relief based upon rescission and the court determines that the contract has not been rescinded, the court may grant any party to the action any other relief to which he may be entitled under the circumstances. A claim for damages is not inconsistent with a claim for relief based upon rescission. The aggrieved party shall be awarded complete relief, including restitution of benefits, if any, conferred by him as a result of the transaction and any consequential damages to which he is entitled; but su
perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates that upon termination due to force majeure, 'all payments made shall become immediately due and payable.' The modified contract omits this requirement. This omission contradicts the principle outlined in California Civil Code § 1692, as highlighted by the Law Explanation. Snippet 1 indicates that upon rescission of a contract, a party may seek relief to recover any money owing to them, implying a restoration of benefits. Removing the clause mandating immediate payment upon termination weakens this principle, potentially allowing a party to avoid paying for services already rendered, which conflicts with the intent of restoring benefits conferred as a result of the transaction.

perturbation.original_text: g. Governing Law and Forum. This Agreement will for all purposes be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of California without giving effect to any conflict of laws principles that require the application of the laws of a different state. Each of the parties hereto (i) irrevocably agrees that the federal and state courts in the Northern District of California shall have sole and exclusive jurisdiction over any suit or other proceeding arising out of or based upon this Agreement, (ii) submits to the venue and jurisdiction of such courts, and (iii) irrevocably consents to personal jurisdiction by such courts.
perturbation.changed_text: g. Governing Law and Forum. This Agreement will for all purposes be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of California without giving effect to any conflict of laws principles that require the application of the laws of a different state. Each of the parties hereto (i) irrevocably agrees that the state courts in the Northern District of California shall have sole and exclusive jurisdiction over any suit or other proceeding arising out of or based upon this Agreement, (ii) submits to the venue and jurisdiction of such courts, and (iii) irrevocably consents to personal jurisdiction by such courts.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'federal' from the jurisdiction clause, the modified contract attempts to limit jurisdiction to only state courts, potentially violating the right of parties to pursue claims in federal court if the requirements for federal jurisdiction (e.g., diversity of citizenship and amount in controversy) are met. This conflicts with federal law establishing federal court jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1332 grants federal courts jurisdiction over civil actions where the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000 and there is diversity of citizenship between the parties. The original clause acknowledged the potential for federal jurisdiction. By restricting jurisdiction solely to state courts, the modified clause attempts to circumvent federal law and deprive parties of their right to a federal forum when the requirements of 28 U.S.C. § 1332 are satisfied.

Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between- (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects o
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes 'federal' from the jurisdiction clause, limiting jurisdiction to state courts only. This directly contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1332, which, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, grants federal courts jurisdiction over civil actions exceeding $75,000 with diversity of citizenship. By attempting to restrict jurisdiction to state courts, the modified clause could deprive parties of their right to a federal forum when the requirements of 28 U.S.C. § 1332 are met, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: a. Insurance. Each party agrees to carry liability insurance sufficient to cover the risks posed under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: a. Insurance. Each party agrees to carry liability insurance as they deem necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the insurance clause to allow each party to determine the 'necessary' amount of liability insurance introduces a potential contradiction with California law regarding reasonable business practices. By not explicitly requiring sufficient coverage for the risks posed, the clause opens the door for inadequate insurance, potentially leaving the other party exposed to uncovered liabilities and violating implied duties of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 1203 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract or duty. While not explicitly mandating insurance coverage amounts, allowing a party to subjectively determine the 'necessary' insurance without regard to actual risks could be interpreted as a lack of good faith, especially if the coverage is manifestly insufficient to protect the other party. This becomes a legal contradiction when the agreement intends to have both entities working with each other, with a lack of insurance on one side resulting in a loss for the other.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1203. (a) Whether a transaction in the form of a lease creates a lease or security interest is determined by the facts of each case. (b) A transaction in the form of a lease creates a security interest if the consideration that the lessee is to pay the lessor for the right to possession and use of the goods is an obligation for the term of the lease and is not subject to termination by the lessee, and: (1) the original term of the lease is equal to or greater than the remaining economic life of the goods; (2) the lessee is bound to renew the lease for the remaining economic life of the goods or is bound to become the owner of the goods; (3) the lessee has an option to renew the lease for the remaining economic life of the goods for no additional consideration or for nominal additional consideration upon compliance with the lease agreement; or (4) the lessee has an option to become the owner of the goods for no additional consideration or for nominal additional consideration upon compliance with the lease agreement. (c) A transact
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original contract's intent to ensure sufficient liability insurance. By allowing each party to determine what insurance is 'necessary,' without reference to the actual risks, the change introduces ambiguity and potential for inadequate coverage. The Law Explanation suggests this could be interpreted as a lack of good faith, especially if the coverage is insufficient to protect the other party. The original contract's explicit requirement for 'sufficient' insurance is weakened, creating a loophole where a party could claim minimal insurance meets their subjective 'necessary' threshold, potentially leaving the other party exposed to uncovered liabilities. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address insurance amounts, it references California Commercial Code § 1203, pertaining to an obligation of good faith in contract performance, which the Law Explanation connects to the potentially insufficient insurance coverage.
